abstract: Responsive hydrogels capable of programmable shape-shifting are central to the development of soft robotics, 4D printing, and intelligent biomedical systems. However, creating complex, heterogeneous architectures that can be reconfigured or functionally repurposed remains a significant challenge due to the permanent nature of most covalent crosslinks. Here, we present a strategy for fabricating reconfigurable hydrogel assemblies by employing a photocontrolled metallopolymer adhesive that allows for the reversible "gluing" of distinct hydrogel units. The adhesive mechanism relies on the synergy between metal–ligand coordination (specifically reversible Ru–thioether bonds) and polymer chain entanglement at the interface. This metallopolymer maintains strong adhesion across various environmental conditions, including changes in temperature and pH, which are used to actuate the motion of the assembled structures. Notably, the assemblies can be selectively disassembled via light irradiation—which triggers the dissociation of the coordination bonds—and随后 reconfigured into new functional architectures through a simple heating process. We demonstrate the versatility of this approach by constructing multi-shape transformers and a modular soft robot capable of navigating complex mazes through programmed morphological changes. This method of using stimuli-responsive adhesives to assemble and re-program hydrogel components provides a modular platform for designing next-generation intelligent materials with user-defined, adaptable functions.
